items.mistake: Using regression because the algorithm is called "logistic regression"
items.fix: Treat logistic regression as classification: use cross-entropy loss, evaluate with classification metrics, and interpret outputs as P(class|x).
items.why: The historical name refers to log-odds (a continuous latent variable), not a continuous target y. The deployment output is a class or probability.
items.example: Wrong: report RMSE on 0/1 churn labels with LinearRegression. Right: LogisticRegression + log loss + precision/recall.

items.mistake: Encoding class labels as integers and training with MSE
items.fix: Use one-hot or class-index targets with cross-entropy / softmax for multi-class; reserve numeric regression loss for truly continuous y.
items.why: Integer class IDs impose a false ordering and spacing — the model learns that class 2 is "between" 1 and 3 in magnitude.
items.example: MNIST digits 0–9: use CrossEntropyLoss on class indices, not MSELoss on digit integers.

items.mistake: Binning a continuous target into classes without business need
items.fix: Keep regression when stakeholders need the actual number; only discretize when the action is truly categorical (e.g. pass/fail gate).
items.why: Arbitrary bins throw away information and make models sensitive to where you cut the scale.
items.example: House prices: regress dollars unless the product only shows {affordable, mid, luxury} tiers with fixed definitions.

items.mistake: Evaluating regression with accuracy after rounding
items.fix: Use MAE, RMSE, or R² for regression; use accuracy, F1, or AUC only when the problem is genuinely classification.
items.why: Rounding hides large numeric errors that matter for regression tasks and gives a false sense of quality.
items.example: Temperature forecast: MAE in °C is meaningful; "accurate to nearest degree" masks a systematic 5°C bias.
